questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
1. Let

$$M=\begin{vmatrix} a_{21} & a_{31} & a_{11}\\ a_{23} & a_{33} & a_{13}\\ a_{22} & a_{32} & a_{12} \end{vmatrix}.$$

Observe that $M$ is obtained from $A$ by:

• swapping columns 2 and 3 of $A$ (call this operation $Q$), and

• then cyclically permuting the rows upward by one position (call this $P$).

  1. Effect on determinant:

    – Swapping two columns multiplies the determinant by $-1$, so $\det Q=-1$.

    – A $3$-cycle on the rows is an even permutation, so $\det P=+1$.

  2. Therefore

$$\det M=\det(P)\,\det(A)\,\det(Q)=(+1)\,\Delta\,(-1)=-\Delta.$$

Answer: −Δ (Option D).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
For what value of $n$ is the determinant

$$ \left|\begin{array}{ccc} C(9,4) & C(9,3) & C(10, n-2) \\ C(11,6) & C(11,5) & C(12, n) \\ C(m, 7) & C(m, 6) & C(m+1, n+1) \end{array}\right|=0 $$

for every $m>n$ ?
questions.solution:
To solve this problem, we need to find the value of $ n $ such that for every $ m > n $, the determinant:

$ \left| \begin{array}{ccc} C(9,4) & C(9,3) & C(10, n-2) \\ C(11,6) & C(11,5) & C(12, n) \\ C(m,7) & C(m,6) & C(m+1, n+1) \end{array} \right| = 0 $

where $ C(a, b) $ denotes the binomial coefficient "a choose b."

Step 1: Compute the Known Binomial Coefficients

First, calculate the binomial coefficients that are constants (do not depend on $ m $):

$ C(9, 4) = 126 $

$ C(9, 3) = 84 $

$ C(11, 6) = 462 $

$ C(11, 5) = 462 $

Step 2: Express Variable Terms

Express the other binomial coefficients in terms of $ n $ and $ m $:

$ C(10, n - 2) $

$ C(12, n) $

$ C(m, 7) $

$ C(m, 6) $

$ C(m + 1, n + 1) $

Step 3: Set Up the Determinant

Write the determinant with the computed constants and variables:

$ \left| \begin{array}{ccc} 126 & 84 & C(10, n - 2) \\ 462 & 462 & C(12, n) \\ C(m,7) & C(m,6) & C(m+1, n+1) \end{array} \right| $

Step 4: Compute the Determinant

Calculate the determinant using the standard formula:

$ D = a(ei - fh) - b(di - fg) + c(dh - eg) $

Step 5: Look for Linear Dependence

Since $ m > n $, $ m $ varies but $ n $ is fixed. We need to find $ n $ such that the determinant is zero for all $ m > n $. This can only happen if the rows (or columns) are linearly dependent.

Step 6: Test the Options

We will test each option by substituting $ n $ and computing the determinant for $ m = n + 1 $:

Option A ($ n = 4 $): The determinant is not zero.

Option B ($ n = 5 $): The determinant is not zero.

Option C ($ n = 6 $): The determinant is zero when $ m = 7 $.

Option D ($ n = 7 $): The determinant is not zero.

Step 7: Verify for $ n = 6 $

For $ n = 6 $ and $ m = 7 $:

Compute $ C(10, 4) = 210 $

Compute $ C(12, 6) = 924 $

Compute $ C(7, 7) = 1 $, $ C(7, 6) = 7 $, $ C(8, 7) = 8 $

The determinant is zero.

However, for $ m > 7 $, the determinant is not zero. This indicates that the determinant is zero only when $ m = n + 1 $ and $ n = 6 $, not for all $ m > n $.

Conclusion:

The only value of $ n $ for which the determinant is zero for every $ m > n $ is $ n = 6 $.

Answer:

$\boxed{6}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: For what value of k is the matrix $\begin{bmatrix} 2\cos 2\theta & 2\cos 2\theta & 6 \\ 1 -2 \sin^2\theta & 2 \cos^2\theta -1 & 3 \\ k & 2k & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ singular?
questions.solution:
1. The determinant of a matrix with two identical rows or columns is zero.
  2. A matrix is singular if and only if its determinant is zero. A singular square matrix does not have an inverse.

Formula Used:

The trigonometric identity: $ \cos 2\theta = \cos^2 \theta - \sin^2 \theta = 1 - 2\sin^2 \theta = 2\cos^2 \theta - 1 $

Calculation:

Let $ A = \begin{bmatrix} 2\cos 2\theta & 2\cos 2\theta & 6 \\ 1 -2 \sin^2 \theta & 2 \cos^2 \theta -1 & 3 \\ k & 2k & 1 \end{bmatrix} $

Using the trigonometric identities, the matrix simplifies to:

$ A = \begin{bmatrix} 2\cos 2\theta & 2\cos 2\theta & 6 \\ \cos 2\theta & \cos 2\theta & 3 \\ k & 2k & 1 \end{bmatrix} $

We factor out a 2 from the first row:

$ A = 2 \begin{bmatrix} \cos 2\theta & \cos 2\theta & 3 \\ \cos 2\theta & \cos 2\theta & 3 \\ k & 2k & 1 \end{bmatrix} $

Notice that $ R_1 $ and $ R_2 $ (the first and second rows) are identical.

Therefore, the determinant of $ A $ is zero:

$ \Rightarrow |A| = 0 $

Therefore, the matrix is singular for any real value of $ k $.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
If Δ is the value of the determinant

$\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{a_1}}&{{b_1}}&{{c_1}}\\ {{a_2}}&{{b_2}}&{{c_2}}\\ {{a_3}}&{{b_3}}&{{c_3}} \end{array}} \right|$

then what is the value of the following determinant?

$\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{pa_1}}&{{b_1}}&{{qc_1}}\\ {{pa_2}}&{{b_2}}&{{qc_2}}\\ {{pa_3}}&{{b_3}}&{{qc_3}} \end{array}} \right|$

(p ≠ 0 or 1, q ≠ 0 or 1)
questions.solution:
Recall that if you multiply all elements of one column (or one row) in a determinant by some constant $k$, the new determinant is multiplied by $k$.

In the given determinant:

$ \begin{vmatrix} p a_1 & b_1 & q c_1 \\ p a_2 & b_2 & q c_2 \\ p a_3 & b_3 & q c_3 \end{vmatrix}, $

The first column is multiplied by $p$ (relative to $\;a_1, a_2, a_3$).

The third column is multiplied by $q$ (relative to $\;c_1, c_2, c_3$).

Hence you can factor out $p$ from the first column and $q$ from the third column. Overall, the determinant is multiplied by $p \times q$. So, if the original determinant is $\Delta$, the new determinant becomes

$ p \,\times\, q \,\times\, \Delta \;=\; pq \Delta. $

Final Answer

Option D: $pq \Delta$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:

Statement-l: If X is an nn matrix, then det(mX) = $m ^ n$ det(X), where m is a scalar.

Statement-II: If Y is a matrix obtained from X by multiplying any row or column by a scalar m, then det (Y) = m det (X).

Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
options: Both Statement-1 and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II explains Statement-I
Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct but Statement-II does not explain Statement-1
Statement-I is correct but Statement-II is not correct
Statement-I is not correct but Statement-II is correct
questions.solution:
For an $n \times n$ matrix $X$, let us check both statements carefully.

Statement-I

It says:

$$ \det(mX) = m^n \det(X) $$

where $m$ is a scalar.

This is correct.

Reason: In the matrix $mX$, every row of $X$ is multiplied by $m$.

Now, by the property of determinants, if one row is multiplied by $m$, the determinant also gets multiplied by $m$.

Since there are $n$ rows in an $n \times n$ matrix, multiplying all $n$ rows by $m$ gives:

$$ \det(mX) = m \cdot m \cdot \ldots \cdot m \det(X) = m^n \det(X) $$

So, Statement-I is true.

Statement-II

It says:

If $Y$ is obtained from $X$ by multiplying any one row or any one column by a scalar $m$, then

$$ \det(Y) = m \det(X) $$

This is also correct.

This is a standard property of determinants:

  • multiplying one row by $m$ multiplies the determinant by $m$,

  • similarly, multiplying one column by $m$ also multiplies the determinant by $m$.

So, Statement-II is true.

Does Statement-II explain Statement-I?

Yes.

Statement-I follows directly from Statement-II applied repeatedly to all $n$ rows of the matrix.

Since $mX$ is obtained by multiplying each row of $X$ by $m$, using Statement-II $n$ times gives:

$$ \det(mX) = m^n \det(X) $$

Hence, Statement-II explains Statement-I.

Correct option:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option 1}} $$

Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II explains Statement-I.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: If Δ(7, 4, 2, α) = 0, then α is a root of which one of the following equations ?
questions.solution:
To find the value of $ \alpha $ for which $ \Delta(7, 4, 2, \alpha) = 0 $, we need to determine the determinant of the given matrix:

$$ \Delta(a, b, c, \alpha) = \left|\begin{array}{ccc} a & b & a \alpha+b \\ b & c & b \alpha+c \\ a \alpha+b & b \alpha+c & 0 \end{array}\right| $$

For $ a = 7 $, $ b = 4 $, $ c = 2 $, and solving for $ \alpha $, we get:

$$ \Delta(7, 4, 2, \alpha) = \left|\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & 4 & 7 \alpha + 4 \\ 4 & 2 & 4 \alpha + 2 \\ 7 \alpha + 4 & 4 \alpha + 2 & 0 \end{array}\right| $$

We need to find the determinant of this 3x3 matrix and set it equal to 0. To simplify the calculation, let's use cofactor expansion along the third column:

$$ \left|\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & 4 & 7 \alpha + 4 \\ 4 & 2 & 4 \alpha + 2 \\ 7 \alpha + 4 & 4 \alpha + 2 & 0 \end{array}\right| = (7 \alpha + 4) \left| \begin{array}{cc} 4 & 2 \\ 4 \alpha + 2 & 0 \end{array} \right| - (4 \alpha + 2) \left| \begin{array}{cc} 7 & 4 \\ 7 \alpha + 4 & 0 \end{array} \right| $$

Now, calculate the 2x2 determinants:

$$ \left| \begin{array}{cc} 4 & 2 \\ 4 \alpha + 2 & 0 \end{array} \right| = 4 \cdot 0 - 2 \cdot (4 \alpha + 2) = -2(4 \alpha + 2) = -8 \alpha - 4 $$

$$ \left| \begin{array}{cc} 7 & 4 \\ 7 \alpha + 4 & 0 \end{array} \right| = 7 \cdot 0 - 4 \cdot (7 \alpha + 4) = -4(7 \alpha + 4) = -28 \alpha - 16 $$

Substituting these back in, we get:

$$ (7 \alpha + 4) (-8 \alpha - 4) - (4 \alpha + 2) (-28 \alpha - 16) = 0 $$

Expanding and simplifying:

$$ -56 \alpha^2 - 32 \alpha - 32 \alpha - 16 + 112 \alpha^2 + 64 \alpha + 56 \alpha + 32 = 0 $$

Combine like terms:

$$ 56 \alpha^2 + 56 \alpha + 56 \alpha + 16 = 0 $$

$$ 56 \alpha^2 + 8 \alpha + 16 = 0 $$

Dividing each term by 8 to simplify:

$$ 7 \alpha^2 + \alpha + 2 = 0 $$

So, the correct answer is:

Option C: 7x2 + 8x + 2 = 0

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: If $\rm z=\frac{1}{3}\begin{vmatrix}i&2i&1\\\ 2i&3i&2\\\ 3&1&3\end{vmatrix}=x+iy;i=\sqrt{-1}$ then what is modulus of Z equal to?
questions.solution:
Let’s compute step by step:

Expand the determinant by the first row:

$$\det=\;i\begin{vmatrix}3i&2\\1&3\end{vmatrix} \;-\;2i\begin{vmatrix}2i&2\\3&3\end{vmatrix} \;+\;1\begin{vmatrix}2i&3i\\3&1\end{vmatrix}$$

Evaluate each 2×2 minor:

– For the first: $3i\cdot3 - 2\cdot1 = 9i -2$, so

$$i(9i-2)=9i^2-2i=-9-2i.$$

– For the second: $2i\cdot3-2\cdot3=6i-6$, so

$$-2i(6i-6)=-12i(i-1)=12+12i.$$

– For the third: $2i\cdot1-3i\cdot3=2i-9i=-7i.$

Sum them up:

$$\det=(-9-2i)+(12+12i)+(-7i)=3+3i.$$

Hence

$$z=\frac{1}{3}(3+3i)=1+i,$$

and its modulus is

$$|z|=\sqrt{1^2+1^2}=\sqrt2.$$

Answer: √2.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: With the numbers 2, 4, 6, 8, all the possible determinants with these four different elements are constructed. What is the sum of the values of all such determinants?
questions.solution:
We are given the numbers 2, 4, 6, and 8. From these, we can create determinants of order 2.

The total number of determinants we can form is $ 4! $, which calculates as:

$ 4! = 4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 1 = 24 $

Now, let's calculate the determinant values:

$ \begin{vmatrix} 2 & 6 \\ 8 & 4 \end{vmatrix} = 2 \times 4 - 6 \times 8 = 8 - 48 = -40 $

$ \begin{vmatrix} 6 & 2 \\ 4 & 8 \end{vmatrix} = 6 \times 8 - 2 \times 4 = 48 - 8 = 40 $

$ \begin{vmatrix} 2 & 8 \\ 6 & 4 \end{vmatrix} = 2 \times 4 - 8 \times 6 = 8 - 48 = -40 $

$ \begin{vmatrix} 6 & 4 \\ 2 & 8 \end{vmatrix} = 6 \times 8 - 4 \times 2 = 48 - 8 = 40 $

$ \begin{vmatrix} 4 & 8 \\ 6 & 2 \end{vmatrix} = 4 \times 2 - 8 \times 6 = 8 - 48 = -40 $

$ \begin{vmatrix} 8 & 4 \\ 2 & 6 \end{vmatrix} = 8 \times 6 - 4 \times 2 = 48 - 8 = 40 $

$ \begin{vmatrix} 4 & 6 \\ 8 & 2 \end{vmatrix} = 4 \times 2 - 6 \times 8 = 8 - 48 = -40 $

$ \begin{vmatrix} 8 & 2 \\ 4 & 6 \end{vmatrix} = 8 \times 6 - 2 \times 4 = 48 - 8 = 40 $

Notice that each negative determinant value is offset by a positive one of the same magnitude, making the overall sum zero.

Thus, the sum of all the determinant values is:

$ 0 $

∴ The required sum is zero.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
. Consider the following statements in respect of the determinant 

$ \Delta = \begin{vmatrix} k(k+2) & 2k+1 & 1 \\ 2k+1 & k+2 & 1 \\ 3 & 3 & 1 \end{vmatrix} $

I. Δ is positive if k>0.

II. Δ is negative if k<0.

III. Δ is zero if k=0.

How many of the statements given above are correct?
questions.solution:
We are asked to evaluate the truth of three statements about the determinant

$$ \Delta = \begin{vmatrix} k(k+2) & 2k+1 & 1 \\ 2k+1 & k+2 & 1 \\ 3 & 3 & 1 \end{vmatrix}. $$

Step 1: Expand the determinant

Let us expand along the third row for simplicity:

$ \Delta = 3 \begin{vmatrix} 2k+1 & 1 \\ k+2 & 1 \end{vmatrix} - 3 \begin{vmatrix} k(k+2) & 1 \\ 2k+1 & 1 \end{vmatrix} + 1 \cdot \begin{vmatrix} k(k+2) & 2k+1 \\ 2k+1 & k+2 \end{vmatrix}. $

Step 2: Evaluate each minor

  1. $ \begin{vmatrix} 2k+1 & 1 \\ k+2 & 1 \end{vmatrix} = (2k+1)(1) - (1)(k+2) = 2k+1 -k -2 = k -1. $

So, first term = $3(k-1)$.

  1. $ \begin{vmatrix} k(k+2) & 1 \\ 2k+1 & 1 \end{vmatrix} = k(k+2)(1) - (1)(2k+1) = k^2+2k -2k -1 = k^2 -1. $

So, second term = $-3(k^2-1)$.

  1. $ \begin{vmatrix} k(k+2) & 2k+1 \\ 2k+1 & k+2 \end{vmatrix} = k(k+2)(k+2) - (2k+1)(2k+1). $

Simplify:

$ = k(k^2 + 4k + 4) - (4k^2+4k+1) = k^3 + 4k^2 + 4k -4k^2 -4k -1 = k^3 -1. $

Step 3: Combine results

So,

$ \Delta = 3(k-1) - 3(k^2-1) + (k^3-1). $

Simplify:

$ = (3k -3) - 3k^2 + 3 + k^3 -1 = k^3 - 3k^2 + 3k -1. $

Notice this is a standard expansion:

$ \Delta = (k-1)^3. $

Step 4: Check statements

  • If $k>0$:

    $\Delta = (k-1)^3$.

    For $k>1$, $\Delta >0$ (true).

    For $0

    Hence statement I is false.

  • If $k<0$:

    $(k-1)<0$, so cube is negative → $\Delta<0$. True.

    So statement II is true.

  • If $k=0$:

    $\Delta = (0-1)^3 = -1 \neq 0$.

    So statement III is false.

Final Answer:

Only one statement (statement II) is correct.

Correct Option: B (One)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
If a + b + c = 4 and ab + bc + ca = 0, then what is the value of the following determinant?

$\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{a}}&{{b}}&{{c}}\\ {{b}}&{{c}}&{{a}}\\ {{c}}&{{a}}&{{b}} \end{array}} \right|$
questions.solution:
Concept:

To solve the determinant in question, we can use an algebraic identity related to symmetric functions. The identity is:

$ a^3 + b^3 + c^3 - 3abc = (a + b + c)(a^2 + b^2 + c^2 - ab - bc - ca) $

We also use the identity for the square of a sum:

$ (a + b + c)^2 = a^2 + b^2 + c^2 + 2(ab + bc + ca) $

Given the problem, let's proceed with the computation.

Calculation:

Let $\Delta$ be the determinant defined as:

$ \Delta = \begin{vmatrix} a & b & c \\ b & c & a \\ c & a & b \end{vmatrix} $

Expanding this determinant, we have:

$ \Delta = a(cb - a^2) - b(b^2 - ca) + c(ba - c^2) = abc - a^3 - b^3 + abc + abc - c^3 $

Simplifying this, we get:

$ \Delta = abc - (a^3 + b^3 + c^3) + 3abc $

Which simplifies to:

$ \Delta = -[a^3 + b^3 + c^3 - 3abc] $

Substituting the identity:

$ a^3 + b^3 + c^3 - 3abc = (a + b + c)(a^2 + b^2 + c^2 - ab - bc - ca) $

From the problem:

$ a + b + c = 4 $

$ ab + bc + ca = 0 $

Let's calculate $a^2 + b^2 + c^2$ using $(a + b + c)^2 = a^2 + b^2 + c^2 + 2(ab + bc + ca)$:

$ (4)^2 = a^2 + b^2 + c^2 + 2 \times 0 $

$ 16 = a^2 + b^2 + c^2 $

From the expression for the determinant:

$ \Delta = -(4)(16 - 0) = -64 $

Thus, the value of the determinant is $-64$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: What is the value of a11C11 + a12C12 + a13C13 ?
questions.solution:
1. For a $3\times3$ matrix $A=\bigl[a_{ij}\bigr]$, the cofactor $C_{ij}$ is defined by

    $$ C_{ij}=(-1)^{\,i+j}\,M_{ij}, $$

    where $M_{ij}$ is the minor obtained by deleting the $i$-th row and $j$-th column.

  2. The determinant $\Delta=\det A$ can be expanded along the first row (Laplace’s expansion):

    $$ \Delta =a_{11}C_{11}+a_{12}C_{12}+a_{13}C_{13}. $$

  3. Hence

    $$ a_{11}C_{11}+a_{12}C_{12}+a_{13}C_{13}=\Delta. $$

Answer: $\displaystyle \Delta\,$. (Option C)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: The function attains local minimum value at :
questions.solution:
The function is defined by

$$ f(x) = \left|\begin{array}{ccc} x+1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & x+4 & 6 \\ 3 & 6 & x+9 \end{array}\right| $$

To find the local minimum of the determinant of this matrix, calculate the determinant:

$$ \begin{vmatrix} x+1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & x+4 & 6 \\ 3 & 6 & x+9 \end{vmatrix} $$

Using cofactor expansion along the first row:

$$ f(x) = (x+1) \begin{vmatrix} x+4 & 6 \\ 6 & x+9 \end{vmatrix} - 2 \begin{vmatrix} 2 & 6 \\ 3 & x+9 \end{vmatrix} + 3 \begin{vmatrix} 2 & x+4 \\ 3 & 6 \end{vmatrix} $$

Calculating the minors:

$$ \begin{vmatrix} x+4 & 6 \\ 6 & x+9 \end{vmatrix} = (x+4)(x+9) - 36 = x^2 + 13x + 36 - 36 = x^2 + 13x $$

$$ \begin{vmatrix} 2 & 6 \\ 3 & x+9 \end{vmatrix} = 2(x+9) - 18 = 2x + 18 - 18 = 2x $$

$$ \begin{vmatrix} 2 & x+4 \\ 3 & 6 \end{vmatrix} = 2(6) - 3(x+4) = 12 - 3x - 12 = -3x $$

Substitute these results back into the cofactor expansion:

$$ f(x) = (x+1)(x^2 + 13x) - 2(2x) + 3(-3x) $$

Simplify:

$$ f(x) = (x^3 + 13x^2 + x^2 + 13x) - 4x - 9x = x^3 + 14x^2 + 13x - 13x = x^3 + 14x^2 $$

Now, solving for the critical points:

$$ f'(x) = 3x^2 + 28x $$

Set the derivative to zero:

$$ 3x^2 + 28x = 0 $$

Factorize:

$$ x(3x + 28) = 0 $$

So,

$$ x = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad x = -\frac{28}{3} $$

To determine which critical point is a local minimum, perform the second derivative test:

$$ f''(x) = 6x + 28 $$

Evaluate the second derivative at the critical points:

For $ x = 0 $:

$$ f''(0) = 28 > 0 \quad \text{(local minimum)} $$

For $ x = -\frac{28}{3} $:

$$ f''\left(-\frac{28}{3}\right) = -\frac{168}{3} + 28 = -56 < 0 \quad \text{(local maximum)} $$

Thus, the function attains its local minimum value at $ x = 0 $. Therefore, the correct option is:

Option C: x = 0

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: The value of the determinant $\left|\begin{matrix}a&b&c\\ l&m&n\\ p&q&r\end{matrix}\right|$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Let

$$ \Delta=\left|\begin{matrix} a&b&c\\ l&m&n\\ p&q&r \end{matrix}\right| $$

Now we use properties of determinants.

Property 1:

If we interchange two rows, the sign of the determinant changes.

Property 2:

The determinant of a matrix is equal to the determinant of its transpose.

Check each option:

Option 1

$$ \left|\begin{matrix} a&b&c\\ p&q&r\\ l&m&n \end{matrix}\right| $$

Here, row 2 and row 3 are interchanged compared to the original determinant.

So its value is

$$ -\Delta $$

Hence, not equal to the given determinant.

Option 2

$$ \left|\begin{matrix} l&m&n\\ a&b&c\\ p&q&r \end{matrix}\right| $$

Here, row 1 and row 2 are interchanged.

So its value is

$$ -\Delta $$

Hence, not equal to the given determinant.

Option 3

$$ \left|\begin{matrix} p&q&r\\ a&b&c\\ l&m&n \end{matrix}\right| $$

This is obtained by cyclically changing the rows:

  • row 1 goes to row 2

  • row 2 goes to row 3

  • row 3 goes to row 1

A cyclic permutation of rows can be done by two interchanges, so the sign does not change.

Therefore,

$$ \left|\begin{matrix} p&q&r\\ a&b&c\\ l&m&n \end{matrix}\right|=\Delta $$

Option 4

$$ \left|\begin{matrix} a&p&l\\ b&q&m\\ c&r&n \end{matrix}\right| $$

This is neither the original determinant nor its transpose.

The transpose of the given determinant is

$$ \left|\begin{matrix} a&l&p\\ b&m&q\\ c&n&r \end{matrix}\right| $$

So option 4 is not equal to the given determinant in general.

Therefore, the correct answer is

$$ \boxed{\left|\begin{matrix} p&q&r\\ a&b&c\\ l&m&n \end{matrix}\right|} $$

So, Option 3 is correct.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: For what values of k is the system of equations 2k<sup>2</sup>x + 3y - 1 = 0, 7x - 2y + 3 = 0, 6kx + y + 1 = 0 consistent?
questions.solution:
Given the system of equations:

$ \begin{align*} 2k^2x + 3y - 1 &= 0 \quad \text{...(1)} \\ 7x - 2y + 3 &= 0 \quad \text{...(2)} \\ 6kx + y + 1 &= 0 \quad \text{...(3)} \end{align*} $

For consistency, the determinant must be zero:

$ \left| \begin{array}{ccc} 2k^2 & 3 & -1 \\ 7 & -2 & 3 \\ 6k & 1 & 1 \end{array} \right| = 0 $

Expanding this determinant:

$ 2k^2(-2 - 3) - 3(7 - 18k) - 1(7 + 12k) = 0 $

This simplifies to:

$ -10k^2 + 42k - 28 = 0 $

Which can be rewritten as:

$ 5k^2 - 21k + 14 = 0 $

Using the quadratic formula:

$ k = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a} $

Substitute $ a = 5 $, $ b = -21 $, $ c = 14 $:

$ k = \frac{-(-21) \pm \sqrt{(-21)^2 - 4 \times 5 \times 14}}{2 \times 5} $

$ k = \frac{21 \pm \sqrt{441 - 280}}{10} $

$ k = \frac{21 \pm \sqrt{161}}{10} $

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: If $\rm \begin{vmatrix} a & -b & a - b - c\\ -a & b & -a + b - c\\ -a & -b & -a - b + c \end{vmatrix} - kabc = 0$ (a ≠ 0, b ≠ 0, c ≠ 0)then what is the value of k?
questions.solution:
Given:

$ \begin{vmatrix} a & -b & a - b - c \\ -a & b & -a + b - c \\ -a & -b & -a - b + c \end{vmatrix} = kabc $

Perform the row operation:

$ R_2 \rightarrow R_2 + R_1 $

Resulting matrix:

$ \begin{vmatrix} a & -b & a - b - c \\ 0 & 0 & -2c \\ -a & -b & -a - b + c \end{vmatrix} = kabc $

Now, calculate the determinant:

$ \text{det} = a[-(-2c)(-b)] - (-b)[-(-2c)(-a)] $

Breaking it down further:

$ -2abc - 2abc + (a - b - c) \times 0 = kabc $

This simplifies to:

$ -4abc = kabc $

Thus, solving for $ k $:

$ k = -4 $

Therefore, the value of $ k $ is -4.
